Name: spectral_stability_bounds_for_latency_critical_inference
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Latency-Critical Inference: A PAC-Bayes Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks to provide rigorous guarantees on the trade-off between accuracy and latency.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization methods for efficient neural network inference. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods or require significant computational overhead. Our proposal differs from these works by leveraging spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to provide rigorous guarantees on the trade-off between accuracy and latency, without requiring significant computational overhead.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to latency-critical inference, leveraging spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols. Our approach provides rigorous guarantees on the trade-off between accuracy and latency, without requiring significant computational overhead. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a range of benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of latency and accuracy.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed approach on a range of benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10
Compare the performance of our approach with state-of-the-art methods, including SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ
Investigate the effect of different spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks on the trade-off between accuracy and latency
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may require significant mathematical derivations and theoretical analysis
The approach may not be applicable to all types of neural networks or datasets
The computational overhead of the approach may be higher than expected, despite the theoretical guarantees
